message.content:
Research depthai XLink errors and firmware/boot behavior for an OAK-D-LITE on Raspberry Pi 4 / ROS 2 Humble.

Use WebSearch and WebFetch (load via ToolSearch "select:WebSearch,WebFetch"). Search depthai-core and depthai-ros GitHub issues, Luxonis docs/discuss forum.

Investigate:
1. "X_LINK_ERROR", "Communication exception", "sys_logger_queue", "No data on logger queue!" — what each means, what triggers them, and which are fatal vs recoverable.
2. XLink watchdog: the default watchdog timeout, what resets it, what happens on timeout (does the device reboot to bootloader 2485?). Env vars DEPTHAI_WATCHDOG, DEPTHAI_WATCHDOG_INITIAL_DELAY, and any others.
3. usbfs memory: usbcore.usbfs_memory_mb — recommended value for depthai (the famous "set to 256/1000") and why; symptoms when too low.
4. udev rules: the Movidius/Luxonis udev rule (80-movidius.rules, SUBSYSTEM=="usb", ATTRS{idVendor}=="03e7", MODE="0666"), why it matters, symptoms when missing.
5. depthai-core reconnection / device.reboot() / auto-recovery behavior, and DEPTHAI_LEVEL/logging to capture failure detail.

Return a detailed markdown section with concrete fixes, exact env var / sysctl / udev values, and citations (URLs).

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

I found relevant information about this error. The error involves a communication exception where the system cannot read data from the 'sys_logger_queue' stream (X_LINK_ERROR) and results in "No data on logger queue" diagnostic errors.

This appears to be a known issue in the DepthAI ROS driver from the Luxonis project. The error manifests as a camera diagnostics error indicating a possible device error or misconfiguration, with the original message stating "Couldn't read data from stream: 'sys_logger_queue' (X_LINK_ERROR)".

The issue was reported on the Luxonis depthai-ros GitHub repository (Issue #575) in August 2024. The error typically indicates a communication problem between the host and the OAK device, potentially related to the device connection or firmware communication channels.

Based on the search results, I found relevant information about the `usbcore.usbfs_memory_mb` parameter in relation to USB camera connections on Raspberry Pi.

The parameter `usbcore.usbfs_memory_mb` can be modified by appending it to the `/boot/firmware/cmdline.txt` file. You can verify the changes have been applied by running: `cat /sys/module/usbcore/parameters/usbfs_memory_mb`.

Regarding OAK-D USB errors on Raspberry Pi:

One common issue is that the OAK Camera draws more current than the RPi4, which can cause crashes. Additionally, DepthAI can throw errors like X_LINK_COMMUNICATION_NOT_OPEN or X_LINK_ERROR, which is usually a sign of a bad USB3 cable (or a USB2 cable).

The specific values you mentioned (256 and 1000) for `usbfs_memory_mb` represent the amount of memory allocated for USB file system operations. Setting this parameter can help resolve certain USB communication issues with devices like the OAK-D camera when connected to Raspberry Pi systems.

Links: [{"title":"Device","url":"https://docs.luxonis.com/software-v3/depthai/depthai-components/device/"},{"title":"Device — DepthAI documentation | Luxonis","url":"https://docs.luxonis.com/projects/api/en/latest/components/device/"},{"title":"CHANGELOG — depthai 2.31.1 documentation","url":"https://docs.ros.org/en/jazzy/p/depthai/__CHANGELOG.html"},{"title":"GitHub - luxonis/depthai-core-v3-release · GitHub","url":"https://github.com/luxonis/depthai-core-v3-release"},{"title":"Getting started with OAK PoE devices — DepthAI Hardware Documentation 1.0.0 documentation","url":"https://docs.luxonis.com/projects/hardware/en/latest/pages/guides/getting-started-with-poe.html"},{"title":"[BUG] Watchdog is not disabled in bootloader · Issue #794 · luxonis/depthai-core","url":"https://github.com/luxonis/depthai-core/issues/794"},{"title":"depthai: DeviceBootloader.cpp Source File","url":"http://docs.ros.org/en/noetic/api/depthai/html/DeviceBootloader_8cpp_source.html"},{"title":"(re)start and watchdog timing is fragile; leads to device not found and startup failures · Issue #291 · luxonis/depthai-core","url":"https://github.com/luxonis/depthai-core/issues/291"}]

Based on the search results, here's what I found about DepthAI watchdog configuration:

## Watchdog Overview

The watchdog is a crucial component in the operation of POE (Power over Ethernet) devices with DepthAI. When DepthAI disconnects from a POE device, the watchdog mechanism is the first to respond, initiating a reset of the camera. This reset is followed by a complete system reboot, which includes the loading of the DepthAI bootloader and the initialization of the entire networking stack.

## Timeout Configuration

You can set the environment variables DEPTHAI_WATCHDOG_INITIAL_DELAY and DEPTHAI_BOOTUP_TIMEOUT to your desired timeout values (in milliseconds). Alternatively, you can set the timeout directly in your code using BoardConfig object with watchdogInitialDelayMs and watchdogTimeoutMs parameters.

## Environment Variables

Key environment variables include:

- DEPTHAI_WATCHDOG_INITIAL_DELAY: Specifies delay after which the device watchdog starts.
- DEPTHAI_WATCHDOG: Useful for debugging (DEPTHAI_WATCHDOG=0), to prevent device reset while the process is paused.
- DEPTHAI_BOOTUP_TIMEOUT: Specifies timeout in milliseconds for waiting the device to boot after sending the binary.

The DEPTHAI_WATCHDOG_INITIAL_DELAY environment variable has a default of 8s for USB and 15s for ETH.

# OAK-D-PRO-POE Device Shutdown Issue #575

## Error Messages
The device exhibited these exact errors before shutting down:

- **Monitor warning**: "ping was missed, closing the device connection"
- **Primary error**: "No data on logger queue!"

- **Technical error**: "Couldn't read data from stream: 'sys_logger_queue' (X_LINK_ERROR)"

Based on the search results, this is a common issue with DepthAI USB devices on Linux systems. Here's what you need to know:

## The Problem

Users haven't set udev rules on their Linux machine, which causes the device to not be found. Without proper permissions, depthai will throw an error like "Insufficient permissions to communicate with X_LINK_BOOTED device".

## The Solution

To fix this, set the udev rules using the commands below, unplugging DepthAI and then plugging it back into USB afterwards:

echo 'SUBSYSTEM=="usb", ATTRS{idVendor}=="03e7", MODE="0666"' | sudo tee /etc/udev/rules.d/80-movidius.rules sudo udevadm control --reload-rules && sudo udevadm trigger

## Troubleshooting Steps

1. Execute lsusb | grep 03e7 to verify the device is recognized (you should see "Intel Movidius MyriadX")
2. After running the udev rules commands, either reboot your system or physically disconnect and reconnect the USB device
3. If you have other offending rules files, simply changing the octal permissions fixed the issue

The file `/etc/udev/rules.d/80-movidius.rules` sets the proper permissions (MODE="0666") for the device with vendor ID 03e7, which is the Intel Movidius device used in OAK cameras.

Links: [{"title":"[BUG] Watchdog is not disabled in bootloader · Issue #794 · luxonis/depthai-core","url":"https://github.com/luxonis/depthai-core/issues/794"},{"title":"Device — DepthAI documentation | Luxonis","url":"https://docs.luxonis.com/projects/api/en/latest/components/device/"},{"title":"CHANGELOG — depthai 2.31.1 documentation","url":"https://docs.ros.org/en/jazzy/p/depthai/__CHANGELOG.html"},{"title":"[BUG] OAK-1 in WSL2/ Ubuntu boot timeout too short · Issue #723 · luxonis/depthai","url":"https://github.com/luxonis/depthai/issues/723"},{"title":"depthai: DeviceBootloader.cpp Source File","url":"http://docs.ros.org/en/noetic/api/depthai/html/DeviceBootloader_8cpp_source.html"},{"title":"Troubleshooting — DepthAI documentation | Luxonis","url":"https://docs.luxonis.com/en/latest/pages/troubleshooting/"},{"title":"(re)start and watchdog timing is fragile; leads to device not found and startup failures · Issue #291 · luxonis/depthai-core","url":"https://github.com/luxonis/depthai-core/issues/291"},{"title":"[BUG] error message: X_LINK_DEVICE_NOT_FOUND · Issue #518 · luxonis/depthai","url":"https://github.com/luxonis/depthai/issues/518"},{"title":"[BUG] Failed to find device after booting, error message: X_LINK_DEVICE_NOT_FOUND · Issue #560 · luxonis/depthai-ros","url":"https://github.com/luxonis/depthai-ros/issues/560"}]

Based on the search results, I found information related to your query about DepthAI watchdog timeout issues:

The default value for POE devices is 4000 ms, and users can set it to 4500ms (max value) for the watchdog timeout.

When connecting to a device over XLink, a bootloader is flashed. If a device connection has high latency, it will fail during that part of the connection regardless of whether the DEPTHAI_WATCHDOG environment was set to a more conservative value.

The watchdog is a crucial component in the operation of POE devices with DepthAI. When DepthAI disconnects from a POE device, the watchdog mechanism initiates a reset of the camera, which is followed by a complete system reboot, including the loading of the DepthAI bootloader and initialization of the entire networking stack.

You can adjust the watchdog timeout by setting the environmental variable DEPTHAI_WATCHDOG (for example: DEPTHAI_WATCHDOG=4500 python3 my_app.py on Linux).

The search results primarily focus on POE (Power over Ethernet) devices, but the underlying watchdog and USB X_LINK reconnection concepts apply to device reboot scenarios. If you're experiencing device reboots or USB reconnection issues, increasing the watchdog timeout value may help, particularly if you have high-latency connections or slower bootloader flashing scenarios.

content.content:
# Watchdog Timeout Analysis from Issue #794

Based on the GitHub issue content provided, here's what can be extracted:

## Default Watchdog Timeout
The issue does not explicitly state the default watchdog timeout value.

## What Resets It
The issue indicates that environment variables can modify watchdog timeouts, but the specific reset mechanism is not detailed in the provided content.

## Watchdog Timeout Behavior
The log shows: `"[2023-03-18 12:19:57.595] [warning] Monitor thread (device: REMOVED [192.168.1.162]) - ping was missed, closing the device connection"` - suggesting the watchdog monitors connection health and closes connections when pings are missed.

## Environment Variables
- **DEPTHAI_WATCHDOG**: Controls watchdog timeout (can be set to 0 to disable)
- **DEPTHAI_WATCHDOG_INITIAL_DELAY**: Referenced in BoardConfig but not explained in detail

The issue notes: "these variables only modify the watchdog timeouts [in] DeviceBase.cpp, but not in DeviceBootloader.cpp."

## Maintainers' Position
The maintainers have not yet responded in this issue. However, a user provided a potential solution link suggesting awareness of the problem, indicating this represents a legitimate bug that needs bootloader watchdog handling improvements for high-latency connections.

Based on the search results, here's what I found about the DepthAI USB device 03e7:2485 (Movidius MyriadX):

## Device Identification

The USB ID 03e7:2485 represents an Intel Movidius MyriadX device that appears when checking with lsusb | grep 03e7.

## Bootloader and Reset Behavior

When the device is waiting for connection, lsusb -v shows USB speed of 480 Mb/s, and after connecting to the device, depthai will upload the firmware, and the speed will change to 5 Gb/s (if USB3 is available).

If the bootloader is flashed, the device can enumerate as the bootloader first, appearing as ID 03e7:f63c (Intel Luxonis Bootloader).

## Device Enumeration During Operation

When running DepthAI applications, the camera goes through a reboot as part of the internal handshake. During that reboot, the camera detaches from USB and reattaches after the reboot.

## Linux Configuration

For Linux systems, you need to define udev rules with: `echo 'SUBSYSTEM=="usb", ATTRS{idVendor}=="03e7", MODE="0666"' | sudo tee /etc/udev/rules.d/80-movidius.rules` followed by `sudo udevadm control --reload-rules && sudo udevadm trigger`.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

content.content:
# OAK USB Deployment: Key Technical Details

## usbcore.usbfs_memory_mb
The provided documentation does not mention `usbcore.usbfs_memory_mb` or its recommended values.

## udev Rules for Vendor 03e7
Linux systems require a udev rule to grant non-root USB device access. The necessary command is:

```
echo 'SUBSYSTEM=="usb", ATTRS{idVendor}=="03e7", MODE="0666"' | sudo tee /etc/udev/rules.d/80-movidius.rules
```

Without this, users encounter the warning: *"Insufficient permissions to communicate with X_LINK_BOOTED device"*

## USB2 vs USB3 Speed Control

**USB3 (Default):** ~5 Gbps capability; *"Only the booted device enumerates as USB3"*

**USB2 Mode:** Forced via Python by setting `maxUsbSpeed=dai.UsbSpeed.HIGH` when creating the `dai.Device` object. This *"upload[s] a USB2-version of the firmware to the device, and will limit the communication speed to USB2."*

**When to force USB2:** Long cables (>2.5m), bad USB3 cables, or USB2-only ports experiencing errors like `X_LINK_COMMUNICATION_NOT_OPEN`.

## Device Reboot/Detach-Reattach Behavior
The documentation describes firmware enumeration stages rather than explicit reboot behavior: devices initially enumerate during bootloading, then re-enumerate after the app boots, switching from USB2 (480 Mb/s) to USB3 (5 Gb/s) speed.

## X_LINK Troubleshooting
- **Root cause:** Typically USB port/cable issues or power delivery problems
- **Solutions:** Try different USB3 ports, shorter cables, or powered USB3 hubs; use screw-locking USB3 cables for secured connections
- **Power considerations:** OAK devices draw 500-900mA; Pro models peak at 15W

Based on the search results, I found relevant information about depthai-ros issues related to your query. Here's what the search revealed:

## Key Findings

The configuration parameter `i_restart_on_diagnostics_error: true` is used in depthai-ros to handle diagnostic errors, which relates to your interest in `restart_on_diagnostics_error`.

There are documented issues with running the ROS2 Humble version of the depthai_ros_driver, and the driver software can be installed via ROS2 Humble repository with depthai_ros_driver version 2.26 or later.

Some users have reported that after running the depthai-ros driver with OAK-D Pro cameras for a period of time (as short as 2 hours or up to 12 hours), the driver stops publishing to rgb and stereo topics, which aligns with your concern about depth publishing stopping.

The Camera node implementation includes diagnostics handling through subscriptions to the "/diagnostics" topic, which relates to the restart_on_diagnostics_error functionality.

The search results indicate there are known connectivity and publishing stability issues in depthai-ros with Humble, particularly around error handling and reconnection scenarios. The GitHub issues repository appears to be where these specific bugs and workarounds are being discussed.

Based on the search results, here's what I found about the DepthAI logging environment variables:

## DEPTHAI_LEVEL Environment Variable

The default global logging level is set to WARN and can be overridden with the ENV variable 'DEPTHAI_LEVEL'.

## Logging Levels Supported

DepthAI supports the following log levels: TRACE, DEBUG, INFO, WARN, ERR, CRITICAL, and OFF.

## X_LINK Logging Control

There is an environment variable that sets logging verbosity of the XLink library, with options including 'debug', 'info', 'warn', 'error', 'fatal' and 'off'.

## Usage Example

To enable debug logging, you can set DEPTHAI_LEVEL to 'debug'. DEPTHAI_LEVEL=trace is required to print pipeline state dumps when pipeline debugging is enabled.

## Debug Output

When setting the DepthAI debugging level to debug (or lower), depthai will also print CPU usage for LeonOS and LeonRT.

content.content:
# DepthAI Troubleshooting Reference

## usbcore.usbfs_memory_mb Value
**Not found in document.** The provided content does not mention this parameter or recommended values.

## 80-movidius.rules udev Command
```bash
echo 'SUBSYSTEM=="usb", ATTRS{idVendor}=="03e7", MODE="0666"' | sudo tee /etc/udev/rules.d/80-movidius.rules
sudo udevadm control --reload-rules && sudo udevadm trigger
```
**Fixes:** Resolves "Failed to find device" and USB permission errors on Linux systems.

## X_LINK Error Codes
**Not comprehensively documented.** The document mentions:
- `X_LINK_DEVICE_NOT_FOUND`: Device enumeration failure, typically resolved by applying udev rules (see above)

Other error codes mentioned (`X_LINK_ERROR`, `X_LINK_COMMUNICATION_NOT_OPEN`, `X_LINK_UNBOOTED`) are **not explained** in this content.

## USB2 Forcing
**Command examples:**
```bash
python3 depthai_demo.py --usbSpeed usb2
python3 depthai_demo.py -usbs usb2
```

**For gen2 API:**
```python
dai.Device(pipeline, usb2Mode=True)
```

**Fixes:** Connection instability, intermittent signal loss with long USB3 cables, and frozen output.

Based on the search results, here's what I found regarding the `usbfs_memory_mb` parameter for OAK camera and DepthAI systems:

## The Command

The buffer memory limit can be changed with the command `echo 1000 > /sys/module/usbcore/parameters/usbfs_memory_mb`, which sets the memory limit to 1000 MB to prevent image acquisition issues.

## Permanent Configuration via cmdline.txt

For a permanent fix on Raspberry Pi systems, open the file `/boot/firmware/cmdline.txt` in your text editor and append `usbcore.usbfs_memory_mb=0` (or replace `0` with your desired value like 256 or 1000). Then reboot the system.

## Alternative for Other Linux Systems

For non-Raspberry Pi systems, add the kernel parameter `usbcore.usbfs_memory_mb=1000` to the bootloader configuration, such as in GRUB or Syslinux.

## Verification

You can verify the changes by running the command `cat /sys/module/usbcore/parameters/usbfs_memory_mb`, which displays the current memory limit.

The value of 0 in some contexts means "unlimited," while 256 or 1000 MB are common fixed memory allocations for high-bandwidth USB camera applications like OAK cameras.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

# Analysis of OAK-D-Lite Crash Issue on RPi4

## Key Information Found:

**Power-Draw Symptoms:**
The user describes "RPI 4B is crashing" when connecting an OAK D-Lite camera via USB 3.0, with the device becoming "bricked." The root cause identified is that "the Oak Camera draws more current than the RPI4."

**Technical Limitation:**
The thread notes "The max export current is 1.2A for RPI4," which is insufficient for the camera's power requirements.

**Solutions Discussed (Not Implemented Details):**

- Employ a powered USB hub for additional current
- Consider an OAK Y Adapter with proper USB 3.0 Type-C to Type-A cables from Luxonis

**Important Note:**

content.text:
I now have all the information needed, well-corroborated across Luxonis docs, GitHub issues, and the RPi forum. Here is the research deliverable.

---

# DepthAI / OAK-D-LITE XLink Errors & Boot Behavior on Raspberry Pi 4 + ROS 2 Humble

**TL;DR.** On the Pi-4 the OAK-D-LITE stalls (the "X_LINK / sys_logger_queue" gotcha #8 in CLAUDE.md) almost always trace to one of three host-side causes: (a) the `usbfs` ring buffer is too small for the high-bandwidth depth stream → set `usbcore.usbfs_memory_mb=256` (or higher); (b) USB power/cable marginality on USB3 → you already force USB2 (`i_usb_speed:=HIGH`), which is correct; (c) the XLink **watchdog** (default **4 s** on USB) firing when the host stalls (GIL/CPU saturation on the Pi), which makes the device **reboot back to the USB bootloader** and detach/re-attach — exactly the "alive but 0 Hz" symptom. The errors `No data on logger queue!` / `Couldn't read data from stream: 'sys_logger_queue' (X_LINK_ERROR)` / `Communication exception` are all the **same fatal event** (XLink link dropped); recovery requires reconnecting to the device, not just waiting. In depthai-ros, enable `i_restart_on_diagnostics_error: true` to auto-reconnect. Capture detail with `DEPTHAI_LEVEL=debug` (or `trace`) and the XLink log level.

---

## 1. The error family: X_LINK_ERROR / Communication exception / sys_logger_queue / "No data on logger queue!"

These are not independent bugs — they are the **same XLink communication failure** reported by different layers when the host↔device pipe goes down (USB drop, device reboot, or watchdog fire). Observed sequence (depthai-ros #575, #591):

```
[warning] Monitor thread (device: ...) - ping was missed, closing the device connection
[error]   No data on logger queue!
Communication exception - possible device error/misconfiguration
Couldn't read data from stream: 'sys_logger_queue' (X_LINK_ERROR)
```

| Symptom string | Layer / meaning | Fatal vs recoverable |
|---|---|---|
| `ping was missed, closing the device connection` | The host **Monitor/watchdog thread** didn't get a heartbeat in time → it tears down the link. This is the *trigger*, the others are *consequences*. | Fatal to the current `Device` handle. Recoverable only by re-opening the device. |
| `No data on logger queue!` | The internal **`sys_logger_queue`** (device→host log channel) returned nothing because the link is already dead. | Symptom, not root cause. |
| `Communication exception - possible device error/misconfiguration` | depthai-core's generic XLink runtime error wrapper. The "misconfiguration" text is misleading — on a Pi-4 it's almost always USB/power/watchdog, not your pipeline config. | Fatal to the handle. |
| `Couldn't read data from stream: '...' (X_LINK_ERROR)` | Raw XLink read failed because the device is gone (rebooted/detached). | Fatal to the handle. |
| `X_LINK_DEVICE_NOT_FOUND` (on relaunch) | Device hasn't finished re-enumerating after the previous crash/reboot, or udev/permissions block it. | Recoverable: wait for re-enumeration, fix udev, or power-cycle. |
| `X_LINK_COMMUNICATION_NOT_OPEN` | Link couldn't be (re)established — classic **bad/too-long USB3 cable** or USB2-cable-on-USB3-port. | Recoverable by fixing cable / forcing USB2. |
| `X_LINK_UNBOOTED` / "Failed to boot device" | Device found but firmware upload/boot failed (power dip mid-boot, marginal cable). | Recoverable: power-cycle, force USB2, fix power. |

**Key takeaway for this project:** the `sys_logger_queue (X_LINK_ERROR)` you hit is the *downstream* report of a link drop. The thing to fix is *why the link dropped* — on the Pi-4 that's usbfs buffer size, USB power/cable, or the watchdog firing because the host stalled. None of these are device-config errors despite the "misconfiguration" wording.

Sources: [depthai-ros #575](https://github.com/luxonis/depthai-ros/issues/575), [depthai-ros #591](https://github.com/luxonis/depthai-ros/issues/591), [depthai #1177 (X_LINK_UNBOOTED)](https://github.com/luxonis/depthai/issues/1177).

---

## 2. XLink watchdog — timeout, what it does, and the reboot-to-bootloader behavior

**What it is.** Each OAK runs a watchdog. The **host** sends a periodic keep-alive ("ping"); if the **device** doesn't see it within the timeout, the **device resets/reboots**; symmetrically, the host **Monitor thread** closes the connection when *it* misses pings ("ping was missed, closing the device connection").

**Defaults**
- **Watchdog timeout: ~4000 ms** (4 s). POE devices document a default of **4000 ms** with a max of **4500 ms**; USB devices use the same short watchdog.
- **Initial delay (`DEPTHAI_WATCHDOG_INITIAL_DELAY`)**: default **8 s for USB**, **15 s for ETH** — the grace period before the watchdog starts after boot.
- **`DEPTHAI_BOOTUP_TIMEOUT`**: how long the host waits for the device to boot after firmware upload.

**On timeout → the device reboots.** When the watchdog fires (or the host disconnects), the device **resets and reboots, reloading the bootloader**. On USB this means it **detaches and re-enumerates**: it comes back on VID:PID **`03e7:2485` (Movidius MyriadX, unbooted/booted)**, or as **`03e7:f63c` (Luxonis bootloader)** if a flash bootloader is present, then re-uploads firmware (USB2 480 Mb/s → USB3 5 Gb/s if available). This detach/re-attach **is** the "camera_node stays alive but stops publishing (0 Hz)" symptom in CLAUDE.md gotcha #8 — the host-side handle is now talking to a device that just rebooted out from under it.

**Why the Pi-4 triggers it.** The watchdog is only 4 s. If the host process stalls — and gotcha #4 says the Pi-4 is **compute-bound** (SLAM ≈ 1 core, GIL-bound fusion) — the host can miss sending keep-alives for >4 s, the device decides the host is gone, reboots, and the link dies with the `sys_logger_queue` error. This is a very plausible root cause for your intermittent stalls under load.

**Environment variables**

| Variable | Effect | Suggested value on Pi-4 |
|---|---|---|
| `DEPTHAI_WATCHDOG` | Watchdog timeout in **ms**. `DEPTHAI_WATCHDOG=0` **disables** it (use for debugging/paused process so the device won't reboot). | Raise it, e.g. `export DEPTHAI_WATCHDOG=10000` to give a loaded Pi-4 headroom before the device gives up on the host. Set `0` only when debugging. |
| `DEPTHAI_WATCHDOG_INITIAL_DELAY` | ms grace before watchdog starts (default 8 s USB / 15 s ETH). | Increase if boot is slow under load. |
| `DEPTHAI_BOOTUP_TIMEOUT` | ms to wait for device to boot after firmware upload. | Increase if you see `X_LINK_UNBOOTED` / boot-timeout on a busy Pi. |

In code (instead of env): `BoardConfig.watchdogTimeoutMs` and `BoardConfig.watchdogInitialDelayMs`.

**Important caveat (bootloader watchdog bug).** Per [depthai-core #794](https://github.com/luxonis/depthai-core/issues/794), the `DEPTHAI_WATCHDOG*` env vars are honored in `DeviceBase.cpp` but **not in `DeviceBootloader.cpp`** — so during the *bootloader/flash* phase a high-latency or stalled connection can still fail regardless of your watchdog setting. Relevant if you ever flash a bootloader; for plain USB run-from-RAM it's mostly the `DeviceBase` watchdog that matters.

Sources: [Luxonis Device docs](https://docs.luxonis.com/projects/api/en/latest/components/device/), [POE getting-started (watchdog 4000/4500 ms, reboots to bootloader)](https://docs.luxonis.com/projects/hardware/en/latest/pages/guides/getting-started-with-poe.html), [depthai-core #794](https://github.com/luxonis/depthai-core/issues/794), [depthai #723 (boot timeout too short)](https://github.com/luxonis/depthai/issues/723), [DeviceHunt 03e7:2485](https://devicehunt.com/view/type/usb/vendor/03E7/device/2485).

---

## 3. usbfs memory — `usbcore.usbfs_memory_mb`

**The fix.** The Linux `usbfs` per-process bulk-transfer buffer defaults to **16 MB**, which is too small for the OAK's high-bandwidth depth/image streams; when the ring fills you get dropped frames, frozen output, and `X_LINK` read failures. The standard depthai remedy is to raise it to **256 MB** (commonly cited; some camera vendors push **1000 MB**).

**Temporary (this boot):**
```bash
sudo sh -c 'echo 256 > /sys/module/usbcore/parameters/usbfs_memory_mb'
# verify
cat /sys/module/usbcore/parameters/usbfs_memory_mb
```

**Permanent on Raspberry Pi 4** — append to `/boot/firmware/cmdline.txt` (single line, space-separated), then reboot:
```
usbcore.usbfs_memory_mb=256
```
(On non-Pi/GRUB systems add `usbcore.usbfs_memory_mb=256` to the kernel cmdline.) `=0` means "unlimited"; a fixed `256`/`1000` is the usual recommendation.

**Symptoms when too low:** intermittent frame drops, throughput collapse after running a while, `libusb`/`X_LINK` transfer errors, "No data" / stalled streams — consistent with the depthai-ros multi-hour rate-drop reports.

Sources: [Configuring USB-FS for USB3 cameras (echo 1000 → usbfs_memory_mb)](https://takawang.medium.com/configuring-usb-fs-for-usb3-vision-camera-5c5727bd0c3d), [Luxonis OAK USB deployment guide](https://docs.luxonis.com/hardware/platform/deploy/usb-deployment-guide), [Allied Vision Vimba X system settings (usbfs default 16 MB rationale)](https://docs.alliedvision.com/Vimba_X/Vimba_X_DeveloperGuide/settings.html).

---

## 4. udev rules — the Movidius/Luxonis rule

**Why it matters.** OAK = Intel **Movidius MyriadX**, USB vendor **`03e7`**. Linux gives USB device nodes restrictive permissions by default, so without a udev rule depthai can't open the device as a non-root user. The device also **re-enumerates** on every connect (and on every watchdog reboot), so the rule must match the vendor, not a fixed device node.

**The rule + reload (run once, then replug):**
```bash
echo 'SUBSYSTEM=="usb", ATTRS{idVendor}=="03e7", MODE="0666"' | \
  sudo tee /etc/udev/rules.d/80-movidius.rules
sudo udevadm control --reload-rules && sudo udevadm trigger
```

**Symptoms when missing / wrong:**
- `Insufficient permissions to communicate with X_LINK_BOOTED device` (classic missing-rule message).
- `RuntimeError: Failed to find device (ma2480) ... X_LINK_DEVICE_NOT_FOUND`.
- Works as root but not as the normal user; works on first connect but fails after the device reboots and re-enumerates.
- Note: a *bad-permission* existing rule (e.g. wrong octal mode) can also fail the config-manager udev check — fix the mode rather than adding a second rule ([depthai #333](https://github.com/luxonis/depthai/issues/333)).

**Verify enumeration:** `lsusb | grep 03e7` should show "Intel Movidius MyriadX" (`03e7:2485`), or "Luxonis Bootloader" (`03e7:f63c`).

Sources: [Luxonis USB deployment guide (udev cmd + "Insufficient permissions" msg)](https://docs.luxonis.com/hardware/platform/deploy/usb-deployment-guide), [depthai-python #2 (throw proper error when udev missing)](https://github.com/luxonis/depthai-python/issues/2), [depthai #514 (X_LINK_DEVICE_NOT_FOUND ↔ udev)](https://github.com/luxonis/depthai/issues/514), [TurtleBot4 OAK-D udev guide](https://iotdesignshop.com/2022/11/21/adding-udev-rules-for-luxonis-oak-d-depth-camera-to-turtlebot4-lite/), [depthai #333](https://github.com/luxonis/depthai/issues/333).

---

## 5. Reconnection / auto-recovery / `device.reboot()` and logging

**Auto-recovery in depthai-ros (the one to use here).** The driver exposes **`i_restart_on_diagnostics_error: true`** — when the camera node's diagnostics report a communication/X_LINK error, the node tears down and **re-instantiates the device** automatically instead of hanging at 0 Hz. This is the direct, supported answer to gotcha #8 ("recover with SIGTERM-by-PID + relaunch") — it lets the node self-recover without your manual kill+relaunch. (Note: it doesn't fix the *underlying* cause; pair it with the usbfs/USB2/watchdog fixes above. Several users report it doesn't always recover a hard USB drop on the first try — depthai-ros #591.)

Add it to the `camera_node` `--ros-args`/YAML, e.g. `-p camera.i_restart_on_diagnostics_error:=true`, alongside `-p camera.i_enable_diagnostics:=true` so the diagnostics that trigger the restart are actually produced.

**`device.reboot()` (depthai-core).** Forces a device reboot → it detaches and re-enumerates through the bootloader (same path the watchdog uses). Useful to deterministically reset a wedged device before reconnecting, instead of relying on the watchdog or a USB power-cycle. After a reboot you must construct a **new** `Device` (the old handle is dead) and may briefly see `X_LINK_DEVICE_NOT_FOUND` until re-enumeration completes.

**Logging to capture the failure detail:**

| Env var | Levels | Use |
|---|---|---|
| `DEPTHAI_LEVEL` | `trace`, `debug`, `info`, `warn` (default), `err`, `critical`, `off` | `DEPTHAI_LEVEL=debug` → also prints **LeonOS/LeonRT CPU usage** (spot device-side overload); `DEPTHAI_LEVEL=trace` → pipeline state dumps. Set this to capture exactly when/why the link drops. |
| XLink log level (`XLINK_LEVEL`) | `debug`, `info`, `warn`, `error`, `fatal`, `off` | Verbosity of the **XLink** transport layer specifically — the layer that emits the `sys_logger_queue (X_LINK_ERROR)` message. Set to `debug` to see the link teardown. |

Example for a debug run:
```bash
DEPTHAI_LEVEL=debug XLINK_LEVEL=debug DEPTHAI_WATCHDOG=10000 \
ros2 run depthai_ros_driver camera_node --ros-args -r __ns:=/turtlebot468 -r __node:=oakd \
  -p camera.i_usb_speed:=HIGH -p camera.i_enable_diagnostics:=true \
  -p camera.i_restart_on_diagnostics_error:=true   # ...your existing -p flags
```

Sources: [DepthAI debugging tutorial (DEPTHAI_LEVEL levels, LeonOS/LeonRT CPU, trace dumps)](https://docs.oakchina.cn/projects/api/tutorials/debugging.html), [depthai Logging.cpp (DEPTHAI_LEVEL default WARN, env override)](http://docs.ros.org/en/noetic/api/depthai/html/Logging_8cpp_source.html), [depthai-core #380 (DEPTHAI_LEVEL=warn)](https://github.com/luxonis/depthai-core/issues/380), [depthai-ros camera.cpp (diagnostics/restart)](https://github.com/luxonis/depthai-ros/blob/humble/depthai_ros_driver/src/camera.cpp), [depthai-ros #591 (i_restart_on_diagnostics_error in practice)](https://github.com/luxonis/depthai-ros/issues/591).

---

## Concrete checklist for robot 468 (Pi-4, USB2, ROS 2 Humble)

1. **usbfs:** add `usbcore.usbfs_memory_mb=256` to `/boot/firmware/cmdline.txt`, reboot, verify with `cat /sys/module/usbcore/parameters/usbfs_memory_mb`.
2. **udev:** ensure `/etc/udev/rules.d/80-movidius.rules` contains the `03e7 MODE="0666"` rule; `udevadm control --reload-rules && udevadm trigger`; confirm `lsusb | grep 03e7`.
3. **USB2 (already done):** keep `-p camera.i_usb_speed:=HIGH` — correct, given the USB3 boot-loop/power gotcha #2.
4. **Watchdog headroom:** `export DEPTHAI_WATCHDOG=10000` (and optionally `DEPTHAI_WATCHDOG_INITIAL_DELAY=12000`) so a CPU-saturated Pi-4 doesn't trip the 4 s default and force a device reboot. Use `DEPTHAI_WATCHDOG=0` only for debugging.
5. **Auto-recover in ROS:** `-p camera.i_enable_diagnostics:=true -p camera.i_restart_on_diagnostics_error:=true` so the node self-reconnects on `X_LINK_ERROR` instead of sitting at 0 Hz (replaces the manual SIGTERM+relaunch in gotcha #8, though keep that as a fallback).
6. **When it stalls, capture it:** rerun with `DEPTHAI_LEVEL=debug XLINK_LEVEL=debug` to confirm whether it's a watchdog/ping miss (host-stall → raise watchdog) vs a USB drop (cable/power/usbfs).

Relevant project file for wiring these flags: `/home/ubuntu/cs7980-guide-mate/src/guide_mate_explorer/launch/depth_lidar_fusion.launch.py` and the camera bring-up command documented in `/home/ubuntu/cs7980-guide-mate/CLAUDE.md` (and `/home/ubuntu/cs7980-guide-mate/docs/camera.md`).
